html:
II

After a long silence, during which Almayer had moved towards the table and sat down, his head between his hands, staring straight before him, Lingard, who had recommenced walking, cleared his throat and said‚Å†‚Äî

‚ÄúWhat was it you were saying?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh! Yes! You should have seen this settlement that night. I don‚Äôt think anybody went to bed. I walked down to the point, and could see them. They had a big bonfire in the palm grove, and the talk went on there till the morning. When I came back here and sat in the dark verandah in this quiet house I felt so frightfully lonely that I stole in and took the child out of her cot and brought her here into my hammock. If it hadn‚Äôt been for her I am sure I would have gone mad; I felt so utterly alone and helpless. Remember, I hadn‚Äôt heard from you for four months. Didn‚Äôt know whether you were alive or dead. Patalolo would have nothing to do with me. My own men were deserting me like rats do a sinking hulk. That was a black night for me, Captain Lingard. A black night as I sat here not knowing what would happen next. They were so excited and rowdy that I really feared they would come and burn the house over my head. I went and brought my revolver. Laid it loaded on the table. There were such awful yells now and then. Luckily the child slept through it, and seeing her so pretty and peaceful steadied me somehow. Couldn‚Äôt believe there was any violence in this world, looking at her lying so quiet and so unconscious of what went on. But it was very hard. Everything was at an end. You must understand that on that night there was no government in Sambir. Nothing to restrain those fellows. Patalolo had collapsed. I was abandoned by my own people, and all that lot could vent their spite on me if they wanted. They know no gratitude. How many times haven‚Äôt I saved this settlement from starvation? Absolute starvation. Only three months ago I distributed again a lot of rice on credit. There was nothing to eat in this infernal place. They came begging on their knees. There isn‚Äôt a man in Sambir, big or little, who is not in debt to Lingard¬†& Co. Not one. You ought to be satisfied. You always said that was the right policy for us. Well, I carried it out. Ah! Captain Lingard, a policy like that should be backed by loaded rifles‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúYou had them!‚Äù exclaimed Lingard in the midst of his promenade, that went on more rapid as Almayer talked: the headlong tramp of a man hurrying on to do something violent. The verandah was full of dust, oppressive and choking, which rose under the old seaman‚Äôs feet, and made Almayer cough again and again.

‚ÄúYes, I had! Twenty. And not a finger to pull a trigger. It‚Äôs easy to talk,‚Äù he spluttered, his face very red.

Lingard dropped into a chair, and leaned back with one hand stretched out at length upon the table, the other thrown over the back of his seat. The dust settled, and the sun surging above the forest flooded the verandah with a clear light. Almayer got up and busied himself in lowering the split rattan screens that hung between the columns of the verandah.

‚ÄúPhew!‚Äù said Lingard, ‚Äúit will be a hot day. That‚Äôs right, my boy. Keep the sun out. We don‚Äôt want to be roasted alive here.‚Äù

Almayer came back, sat down, and spoke very calmly‚Å†‚Äî

‚ÄúIn the morning I went across to see Patalolo. I took the child with me, of course. I found the water-gate barred, and had to walk round through the bushes. Patalolo received me lying on the floor, in the dark, all the shutters closed. I could get nothing out of him but lamentations and groans. He said you must be dead. That Lakamba was coming now with Abdulla‚Äôs guns to kill everybody. Said he did not mind being killed, as he was an old man, but that the wish of his heart was to make a pilgrimage. He was tired of men‚Äôs ingratitude‚Å†‚Äîhe had no heirs‚Å†‚Äîhe wanted to go to Mecca and die there. He would ask Abdulla to let him go. Then he abused Lakamba‚Å†‚Äîbetween sobs‚Å†‚Äîand you, a little. You prevented him from asking for a flag that would have been respected‚Å†‚Äîhe was right there‚Å†‚Äîand now when his enemies were strong he was weak, and you were not there to help him. When I tried to put some heart into him, telling him he had four big guns‚Å†‚Äîyou know the brass six-pounders you left here last year‚Å†‚Äîand that I would get powder, and that, perhaps, together we could make head against Lakamba, he simply howled at me. No matter which way he turned‚Å†‚Äîhe shrieked‚Å†‚Äîthe white men would be the death of him, while he wanted only to be a pilgrim and be at peace. My belief is,‚Äù added Almayer, after a short pause, and fixing a dull stare upon Lingard, ‚Äúthat the old fool saw this thing coming for a long time, and was not only too frightened to do anything himself, but actually too scared to let you or me know of his suspicions. Another of your particular pets! Well! You have a lucky hand, I must say!‚Äù

Lingard struck a sudden blow on the table with his clenched hand. There was a sharp crack of splitting wood. Almayer started up violently, then fell back in his chair and looked at the table.

‚ÄúThere!‚Äù he said, moodily, ‚Äúyou don‚Äôt know your own strength. This table is completely ruined. The only table I had been able to save from my wife. By and by I will have to eat squatting on the floor like a native.‚Äù

Lingard laughed heartily. ‚ÄúWell then, don‚Äôt nag at me like a woman at a drunken husband!‚Äù He became very serious after awhile, and added, ‚ÄúIf it hadn‚Äôt been for the loss of the Flash I would have been here three months ago, and all would have been well. No use crying over that. Don‚Äôt you be uneasy, Kaspar. We will have everything shipshape here in a very short time.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat? You don‚Äôt mean to expel Abdulla out of here by force! I tell you, you can‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot I!‚Äù exclaimed Lingard. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs all over, I am afraid. Great pity. They will suffer for it. He will squeeze them. Great pity. Damn it! I feel so sorry for them if I had the Flash here I would try force. Eh! Why not? However, the poor Flash is gone, and there is an end of it. Poor old hooker. Hey, Almayer? You made a voyage or two with me. Wasn‚Äôt she a sweet craft? Could make her do anything but talk. She was better than a wife to me. Never scolded. Hey?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ And to think that it should come to this. That I should leave her poor old bones sticking on a reef as though I had been a damned fool of a southern-going man who must have half a mile of water under his keel to be safe! Well! well! It‚Äôs only those who do nothing that make no mistakes, I suppose. But it‚Äôs hard. Hard.‚Äù

He nodded sadly, with his eyes on the ground. Almayer looked at him with growing indignation.

‚ÄúUpon my word, you are heartless,‚Äù he burst out; ‚Äúperfectly heartless‚Å†‚Äîand selfish. It does not seem to strike you‚Å†‚Äîin all that‚Å†‚Äîthat in losing your ship‚Å†‚Äîby your recklessness, I am sure‚Å†‚Äîyou ruin me‚Å†‚Äîus, and my little Nina. What‚Äôs going to become of me and of her? That‚Äôs what I want to know. You brought me here, made me your partner, and now, when everything is gone to the devil‚Å†‚Äîthrough your fault, mind you‚Å†‚Äîyou talk about your ship‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ ship! You can get another. But here. This trade. That‚Äôs gone now, thanks to Willems.‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Your dear Willems!‚Äù

‚ÄúNever you mind about Willems. I will look after him,‚Äù said Lingard, severely. ‚ÄúAnd as to the trade‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ I will make your fortune yet, my boy. Never fear. Have you got any cargo for the schooner that brought me here?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe shed is full of rattans,‚Äù answered Almayer, ‚Äúand I have about eighty tons of guttah in the well. The last lot I ever will have, no doubt,‚Äù he added, bitterly.

‚ÄúSo, after all, there was no robbery. You‚Äôve lost nothing actually. Well, then, you must‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Hallo! What‚Äôs the matter!‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Here!‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúRobbery! No!‚Äù screamed Almayer, throwing up his hands.

He fell back in the chair and his face became purple. A little white foam appeared on his lips and trickled down his chin, while he lay back, showing the whites of his upturned eyes. When he came to himself he saw Lingard standing over him, with an empty water-chatty in his hand.

‚ÄúYou had a fit of some kind,‚Äù said the old seaman with much concern. ‚ÄúWhat is it? You did give me a fright. So very sudden.‚Äù

Almayer, his hair all wet and stuck to his head, as if he had been diving, sat up and gasped.

‚ÄúOutrage! A fiendish outrage. I‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

Lingard put the chatty on the table and looked at him in attentive silence. Almayer passed his hand over his forehead and went on in an unsteady tone:

‚ÄúWhen I remember that, I lose all control,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI told you he anchored Abdulla‚Äôs ship abreast our jetty, but over to the other shore, near the Rajah‚Äôs place. The ship was surrounded with boats. From here it looked as if she had been landed on a raft. Every dugout in Sambir was there. Through my glass I could distinguish the faces of people on the poop‚Å†‚ÄîAbdulla, Willems, Lakamba‚Å†‚Äîeverybody. That old cringing scoundrel Sahamin was there. I could see quite plain. There seemed to be much talk and discussion. Finally I saw a ship‚Äôs boat lowered. Some Arab got into her, and the boat went towards Patalolo‚Äôs landing-place. It seems they had been refused admittance‚Å†‚Äîso they say. I think myself that the water-gate was not unbarred quick enough to please the exalted messenger. At any rate I saw the boat come back almost directly. I was looking on, rather interested, when I saw Willems and some more go forward‚Å†‚Äîvery busy about something there. That woman was also amongst them. Ah, that woman‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

Almayer choked, and seemed on the point of having a relapse, but by a violent effort regained a comparative composure.

‚ÄúAll of a sudden,‚Äù he continued‚Å†‚Äî‚Äúbang! They fired a shot into Patalolo‚Äôs gate, and before I had time to catch my breath‚Å†‚ÄîI was startled, you may believe‚Å†‚Äîthey sent another and burst the gate open. Whereupon, I suppose, they thought they had done enough for a while, and probably felt hungry, for a feast began aft. Abdulla sat amongst them like an idol, cross-legged, his hands on his lap. He‚Äôs too great altogether to eat when others do, but he presided, you see. Willems kept on dodging about forward, aloof from the crowd, and looking at my house through the ship‚Äôs long glass. I could not resist it. I shook my fist at him.‚Äù

‚ÄúJust so,‚Äù said Lingard, gravely. ‚ÄúThat was the thing to do, of course. If you can‚Äôt fight a man the best thing is to exasperate him.‚Äù

Almayer waved his hand in a superior manner, and continued, unmoved: ‚ÄúYou may say what you like. You can‚Äôt realize my feelings. He saw me, and, with his eye still at the small end of the glass, lifted his arm as if answering a hail. I thought my turn to be shot at would come next after Patalolo, so I ran up the Union Jack to the flagstaff in the yard. I had no other protection. There were only three men besides Ali that stuck to me‚Å†‚Äîthree cripples, for that matter, too sick to get away. I would have fought single-handed, I think, I was that angry, but there was the child. What to do with her? Couldn‚Äôt send her up the river with the mother. You know I can‚Äôt trust my wife. I decided to keep very quiet, but to let nobody land on our shore. Private property, that; under a deed from Patalolo. I was within my right‚Å†‚Äîwasn‚Äôt I? The morning was very quiet. After they had a feed on board the barque with Abdulla most of them went home; only the big people remained. Towards three o‚Äôclock Sahamin crossed alone in a small canoe. I went down on our wharf with my gun to speak to him, but didn‚Äôt let him land. The old hypocrite said Abdulla sent greetings and wished to talk with me on business; would I come on board? I said no; I would not. Told him that Abdulla may write and I would answer, but no interview, neither on board his ship nor on shore. I also said that if anybody attempted to land within my fences I would shoot‚Å†‚Äîno matter whom. On that he lifted his hands to heaven, scandalized, and then paddled away pretty smartly‚Å†‚Äîto report, I suppose. An hour or so afterwards I saw Willems land a boat party at the Rajah‚Äôs. It was very quiet. Not a shot was fired, and there was hardly any shouting. They tumbled those brass guns you presented to Patalolo last year down the bank into the river. It‚Äôs deep there close to. The channel runs that way, you know. About five, Willems went back on board, and I saw him join Abdulla by the wheel aft. He talked a lot, swinging his arms about‚Å†‚Äîseemed to explain things‚Å†‚Äîpointed at my house, then down the reach. Finally, just before sunset, they hove upon the cable and dredged the ship down nearly half a mile to the junction of the two branches of the river‚Å†‚Äîwhere she is now, as you might have seen.‚Äù

Lingard nodded.

‚ÄúThat evening, after dark‚Å†‚ÄîI was informed‚Å†‚ÄîAbdulla landed for the first time in Sambir. He was entertained in Sahamin‚Äôs house. I sent Ali to the settlement for news. He returned about nine, and reported that Patalolo was sitting on Abdulla‚Äôs left hand before Sahamin‚Äôs fire. There was a great council. Ali seemed to think that Patalolo was a prisoner, but he was wrong there. They did the trick very neatly. Before midnight everything was arranged as I can make out. Patalolo went back to his demolished stockade, escorted by a dozen boats with torches. It appears he begged Abdulla to let him have a passage in the Lord of the Isles to Penang. From there he would go to Mecca. The firing business was alluded to as a mistake. No doubt it was in a sense. Patalolo never meant resisting. So he is going as soon as the ship is ready for sea. He went on board next day with three women and half a dozen fellows as old as himself. By Abdulla‚Äôs orders he was received with a salute of seven guns, and he has been living on board ever since‚Å†‚Äîfive weeks. I doubt whether he will leave the river alive. At any rate he won‚Äôt live to reach Penang. Lakamba took over all his goods, and gave him a draft on Abdulla‚Äôs house payable in Penang. He is bound to die before he gets there. Don‚Äôt you see?‚Äù

He sat silent for a while in dejected meditation, then went on:

‚ÄúOf course there were several rows during the night. Various fellows took the opportunity of the unsettled state of affairs to pay off old scores and settle old grudges. I passed the night in that chair there, dozing uneasily. Now and then there would be a great tumult and yelling which would make me sit up, revolver in hand. However, nobody was killed. A few broken heads‚Å†‚Äîthat‚Äôs all. Early in the morning Willems caused them to make a fresh move which I must say surprised me not a little. As soon as there was daylight they busied themselves in setting up a flagpole on the space at the other end of the settlement, where Abdulla is having his houses built now. Shortly after sunrise there was a great gathering at the flagpole. All went there. Willems was standing leaning against the mast, one arm over that woman‚Äôs shoulders. They had brought an armchair for Patalolo, and Lakamba stood on the right hand of the old man, who made a speech. Everybody in Sambir was there: women, slaves, children‚Å†‚Äîeverybody! Then Patalolo spoke. He said that by the mercy of the Most High he was going on a pilgrimage. The dearest wish of his heart was to be accomplished. Then, turning to Lakamba, he begged him to rule justly during his‚Å†‚ÄîPatalolo‚Äôs‚Å†‚Äîabsence. There was a bit of playacting there. Lakamba said he was unworthy of the honourable burden, and Patalolo insisted. Poor old fool! It must have been bitter to him. They made him actually entreat that scoundrel. Fancy a man compelled to beg of a robber to despoil him! But the old Rajah was so frightened. Anyway, he did it, and Lakamba accepted at last. Then Willems made a speech to the crowd. Said that on his way to the west the Rajah‚Å†‚Äîhe meant Patalolo‚Å†‚Äîwould see the Great White Ruler in Batavia and obtain his protection for Sambir. Meantime, he went on, I, an Orang Blanda and your friend, hoist the flag under the shadow of which there is safety. With that he ran up a Dutch flag to the masthead. It was made hurriedly, during the night, of cotton stuffs, and, being heavy, hung down the mast, while the crowd stared. Ali told me there was a great sigh of surprise, but not a word was spoken till Lakamba advanced and proclaimed in a loud voice that during all that day everyone passing by the flagstaff must uncover his head and salaam before the emblem.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, hang it all!‚Äù exclaimed Lingard‚Å†‚Äî‚ÄúAbdulla is British!‚Äù

‚ÄúAbdulla wasn‚Äôt there at all‚Å†‚Äîdid not go on shore that day. Yet Ali, who has his wits about him, noticed that the space where the crowd stood was under the guns of the Lord of the Isles. They had put a coir warp ashore, and gave the barque a cant in the current, so as to bring the broadside to bear on the flagstaff. Clever! Eh? But nobody dreamt of resistance. When they recovered from the surprise there was a little quiet jeering; and Bahassoen abused Lakamba violently till one of Lakamba‚Äôs men hit him on the head with a staff. Frightful crack, I am told. Then they left off jeering. Meantime Patalolo went away, and Lakamba sat in the chair at the foot of the flagstaff, while the crowd surged around, as if they could not make up their minds to go. Suddenly there was a great noise behind Lakamba‚Äôs chair. It was that woman, who went for Willems. Ali says she was like a wild beast, but he twisted her wrist and made her grovel in the dust. Nobody knows exactly what it was about. Some say it was about that flag. He carried her off, flung her into a canoe, and went on board Abdulla‚Äôs ship. After that Sahamin was the first to salaam to the flag. Others followed suit. Before noon everything was quiet in the settlement, and Ali came back and told me all this.‚Äù

Almayer drew a long breath. Lingard stretched out his legs.

‚ÄúGo on!‚Äù he said.

Almayer seemed to struggle with himself. At last he spluttered out:

‚ÄúThe hardest is to tell yet. The most unheard-of thing! An outrage! A fiendish outrage!‚Äù
